CHAPTER 95 .
AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 1093 of the Kentucky
Statutes (Carroll's 1930 Edition ) , being an Act defining and
prescribing the jurisdiction of justices and quarterly courts
and the judges of police courts in cities of the first , second ,
third , fourth , fifth and sixth class in this Commonwealth in
penal and misdemeanor cases .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 1093 of the Kentucky Statutes (Car-
roll's 1930 Edition ) be repealed and re -enacted so
that same , when thus re-enacted shall read as fol-
lows :
" That justices , county and quarterly courts and
the judges thereof , and police courts in cities of
the first , second , third , fourth , fifth and sixth class
of this Commonwealth and the judges thereof , shall
have jurisdiction exclusive of circuit courts in ali
penal and misdemeanor cases , the punishment of
which is limited to a fine of not exceeding twenty
dollars , and jurisdiction concurrent with circuit
courts of all penal cases and misdemeanor cases ,
the punishment of which is limited to a fine of not
exceeding five hundred dollars , or imprisonment
not exceeding twelve months , or both ; but said
courts shall also have and exercise the same juris-
diction in the enforcement of city ordinances that
is now given to them by law, "

CHAPTER 96- 423
"Nothing in this act shall abridge on repeal the
right of appeal from any judgment of conviction
as now by law made and provided . "
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 96.
AN ACT to amend and re- enact Section One thousand and fifty-
five of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , relating to records of
quarterly courts and the appointment , powers , duties and com-
pensation of clerks thereof, by providing for the collection of
fees upon the institution of civil actions and proceedings in
the quarterly courts held in counties having a population in
excess of two hundred fifty thousand ( 250,000 ) , fixing the
amount thereof , providing for the application and disposition
of said fees when collected , declaring an emergency to exist ,
and repealing laws and parts of laws in conflict herewith .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
(One) That Section One Thousand and fifty -five
of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes is amended by in-
serting in the said section immediately following the
end of the first paragraph thereof and immediately
preceding the second paragraph thereof the follow-
ing : "The clerk of the quarterly courts held in
counties having a population in excess of two hun-
dred fifty thousand ( 250,000 ) shall collect upon civil
action or civil proceeding commencing with original
process in the said quarterly courts, except upon ap-
peals from the orders and judgments of fiscal courts ,
police courts having civil jurisdiction , and courts
of justices of the peace , the sum of one dollar andfifty cents ($1.50 ) which sum shall be collected when
each civil action or civil proceeding is instituted ,
and shall be applied as a credit upon the costs ac-cruing in the respective civil actions and proceed-
ings, and such funds , after collection , shall be ac-

424 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
counted for by the said clerks and disposed of as
hereinabove provided , " so that when so amended
and re-enacted the said Section One Thousand and
Fifty-five shall read as follows : The judge of the
quarterly court shall keep a record of all his official
acts out of term time , and shall keep a docket, order
book and execution book , in which shall be recorded ,
as clerks are required to do , the proceedings of the
court, and shall fix a rule day for the return of exe-
cutions and other process . The record books shall
be paid for by the county. He may appoint a clerk
of his court, who shall have power to file papers and
issue all process and administer the oaths ; Pro-
vided, that in counties having a population of one
hundred and fifty thousand or more the county judge
elected at a regular election for said office , shall , on
the first Monday in July succeeding his election , ap-
point for a period of four years a clerk and two
deputy clerks of the quarterly court , who shall have
power to file papers and issue all processes , and for
their services the clerk shall be paid at the rate
of three thousand dollars per annum , and each of
the deputy clerks at the rate of fifteen hundred dol-
lars per annum , said salaries to be paid in monthly
installments and by the fiscal court of the county in
which said clerk and deputies are appointed , out of
the county treasury , from money raised for county
purposes , and all fees collected from the quarterly
court which heretofore have been paid to the county
judge in said counties shall be paid into the treasury
of the county. Said collections shall be made by the
clerk, who shall settle monthly with the county judge
and pay the amount found by such settlement in his
hands immediately into the county treasury .
The clerks of the quarterly courts held in coun-
ties having a population in excess of two hundred
fifty thousand (250,000 ) shall collect upon each civil

CHAPTER 96 425
action or civil proceeding commencing with origina !
process in the said quarterly courts , except upon
appeals from the orders and judgments of fiscal
courts , police courts , having civil jurisdiction , and
courts of justices of the peace , the sum of one dol-
lar and fifty cents ( $ 1.50 ) , which sum shall be col-
lected when each civil action or civil proceeding is
instituted , and shall be applied as a credit upon the
costs accruing in the respective civil actions and
proceedings , and such funds , after collection shall
be accounted for by the said clerks and disposed
of as hereinabove provided .
Said clerk and deputies shall , before entering
upon the discharge of their duties , execute bond be-
fore the county court, with security approved by the
court , to the Commonwealth of Kentucky , for the
faithful performance of their duties .
(Two) Whereas , in counties having a popula-
tion in excess of two hundred fifty thousand (250 ,-
000 ) intensive industrial and commercial develop-
ment and consequently density of population have
combined to produce definitely changed conditions
of life, as a result of which the demands upon the
courts of limited jurisdiction in such counties are
continuous , voluminous and exacting , and whereas
it is provided by law that the sum of one dollar
($1.00) shall be collected upon the institution of
each civil action and proceeding in courts of justices
of the peace held therein and no sum is required to
be collected upon the institution of civil actions and
proceedings in quarterly courts thereof, and be-
cause of the foregoing the quarterly courts in such
counties are so over-burdened with a continually
increasing volume of litigation which is within the
concurrent jurisdiction of quarterly courts andjustice's courts therein that the proper administra-
tion of justice in the quarterly courts in such coun-

426 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
ties , is unreasonably delayed and confused and the
justice's courts held in such counties are not over-
burdered with such litigation , an emergency is de-
clared to exist in the said counties , and this Act
shall take effect upon its passage by the General
Assembly and approval by the Governor .
(Three ) All laws and parts of laws in conflict
herewith are , to the extent of the conflict , hereby
repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 97.
AN ACT to authorize the State Librarian to furnish without
cost to the Library of the University of Kentucky and to the
libraries of the four State Teachers Colleges , certain public
documents and books when authorized by the trustees to do
80.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Keniucky :
Section 1. The State Librarian is hereby author-
ized to furnish free of charge to the Library of the
University of Kentucky and to the libraries of the
4 state teachers colleges , and the 2 colored teachers
colleges , such public documents , books and manu-
scripts in the said State Library , as are essential
to the performance of the duties of that Library,
when and as the trustees of the said State Library
may , from time to time at their discretion , author-
ize him to furnish .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 98 .
AN ACT to authorize the Circuit Clerks as Ex -Officio County
Librarians to give the University of Kentucky Library and

CHAPTER 99 427
I
to the libraries of the four State Teachers ' Colleges certain
documents , books and other library materials at their discre-
tion .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. The Circuit Court Clerk of each coun-
ty, as Ex-Officio County Librarian, is hereby au-
thorized at his discretion to give the Library of
the University of Kentucky and to the libraries of
the four State Teachers ' Colleges , such non -current
documents and reports and such other library ma-
terials , not needed by the county , as he may have
in his possession .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 99.
AN ACT to abolish the Department of Library and Archives
created by Article 20 of the Administration Reorganization
Act ; providing that the Kentucky Library Commission be
transferred to the Department of Public Property and be a
division thereof ; creating the offices of Librarian and Law
Librarian ; fixing their salaries and providing for their ap-
pointment ; making the Judges of the Court of Appeals Trus-
tees of the Law Library ; repealing Article 20 of the Admin-
istrative Reorganization Act , being House Bill 428 and Sec-
tions 2439 and 2447 , Carroll's 1933 Supplement to Kentucky
Statutes , and repealing all other laws and parts of laws in
conflict with this Act.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The Department of Library and Archives
created by Article 20 , Section 1 , of the Administra-
tive Reorganization Act is hereby abolished .
§ 2. The Kentucky Library Commission and so
much of the Library of the Commonwealth as isnot embraced in the Law Library are hereby trans-

428 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
ferred to the Department of Public Property and
attached thereto and made a division thereof , and
shall continue to exercise all of the functions , pow-
ers and duties vested in them by law. On the 30th
day of June , 1936 , and every four years there-
after , the Governor shall appoint a Librarian to
serve during the will and pleasure of the Governor ,
who shall receive an annual salary of not exceeding
twenty-four Hundred ( $ 2,400.00 ) Dollars , payable
monthly out of the State Treasury . The Librarian
so appointed shall succeed to and be vested with
all of the powers , duties and functions imposed by
law upon the present Librarian and the Secretary
to the Kentucky Library Commission , except those
duties vested by this Act in the Law Librarian .
§ 3. There is hereby created the office of Law
Librarian , who shall be appointed by the Court of
Appeals on the 30th day of June , 1936 , and every
four years thereafter . The Law Librarian shall
receive an annual salary of Twenty -four Hundred
( $ 2,400.00 ) Dollars , payable monthly out of the
State Treasury . The Law Librarian shall succeed
to and be vested with all the powers , duties and
functions imposed by law upon the present Libra-
rian , except those duties pertaining to so much of
the State Library as is by Section 2 of this Act
transferred to the Department of Public Property .
$ 4. The Judges of the Court of Appeals of
Kentucky and the Attorney General shall be Trus-
tees of the Law Library . They shall see that the
Law Librarian properly discharges the duties of
his office .
§ 5. Article 20 of the Administrative Reorgan-
ization Act , being House Bill 428 , creating the De-
partment of Library and Archives , Sections 2439
and 2447 , Carroll's 1933 Supplement to the Ken-
tucky Statutes , are hereby repealed , and all other

CHAPTER 100 429
laws and parts of laws in conflict with the pro-
visions of this Act are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 100 .
AN ACT to regulate the Department of Mines and Minerals of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky ; to provide for the appoint-
ment and employment of officers and employees thereof ; to
designate the chief officer of said department as the Chief of
the Department of Mines and Minerals ; to prescribe the qual-
ifications of such officers and employees and define their
duties , rights and privileges , and to fix their salaries and pro-
vide for the payment of their expenses ; to provide offices for
said department and necessary and proper equipment , instru-
ments , appliances , apparatus , trucks , chemicals , stationery,
postage , and other things , for the use of said department and
its officers and employees ; to provide for the supervision and
inspection of all commercial coal mines by said Department
of Mines and Minerals of this Commonwealth , and to provide
for the safety and protection of persons employed in and
about mines and of mining property and other property used
in connection with mines ; to prescribe the duties of mine
owners and operators and their employees respecting the
planning , equipment , management and operation of mines
and the methods of work and labor therein and in connec-
tion therewith ; to provide that said department and its offi-
cers and employees shall operate and be protected by the pro-
visions of the Workmen's Compensation Laws of Kentucky ;
to provide penalties for violations of this Act and to repeal
certain statutes and all laws in conflict herewith .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Definitions .
Mine : In this act the term " mine " includes the
shafts , slopes, drifts , or inclined planes connected
with excavations penetrating coal stratum or strata,
which excavations are ventilated by one general air
current , or divisions thereof, and connected by one

430 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
1
general system of mine railroads over which coal
may be delivered to one or more points outside the
mine .
Excavations and Workings : The term " excava-
tions and workings " includes all the excavated por-
tions of the mine , those abandoned as well as the
places actually being worked ; also all underground
workings and shafts , tunnels and other ways and
openings and all such shafts , slopes , tunnels , and
other openings in the course of being sunk or driven ,
together with all roads , appliances , machinery and
material connected with the same below the surface .
Shaft : The term " shaft " means a vertical open-
ing through the strata that is or may be used for the
purpose of ventilation or drainage , or for hoisting
men , coal or material , or either , in connection with
the mining of coal .
Slope : The term " slope " means an inclined
opening used for the same purpose as a shaft .
Drift : The term " drift " means an opening
through the strata , on which opening grades are
such as to permit the coal to be hauled by mules or
mechanical traction power, and which opening may
be used for the purpose of ventilation , drainage , in-
gress , egress , and other purposes in connection with
the mining of coal .
Operator : The term " operator " means any
firm , corporation , or individual operating any coal
mine , or any part thereof.
Superintendent : The term " superintendent'
means the person who shall have on behalf of the
operator, immediate supervision of one or more
mines .
Mine Foreman : The term " mine foreman "
means the person whom the operator or superin-
tendent shall place in charge of the workings of the
mine and of persons employed therein. He is re-

CHAPTER 100 431
quired to be certificated and of seeing that the pro-
visions of the mining law are complied with .
Assistant Mine Foreman : The term " assistant
mine foreman " means the person designated to as-
sist the mine foreman in the immediate supervision
of a portion or the whole of a mine and of the per-
sons employed therein.
Fire Boss : The term " fire boss " means any per-
son whom the operator or superintendent is required
to employ under certain conditions with reference
to explosive gases in this act designated , when same
are found to exist in a mine . He is required to be
certificated and is charged with the duties of pre-
serving the safety of the persons employed in such
mines with reference to ventilation and gases .
Shot -Firers : The term " shot -firers " means a
practical and experienced person whose duty shall
be to charge , set off and dischage the shots in all
blasting in the workings of said mine when deemed
advisable by the operator or superintendent to do
so ; or when ordered to do so in gaseous mines by
the district inspector and approved by the Chief of
the Department of Mines and Minerals . Said shot-
firers shall at all times be under the direction of
the mine foreman .
District Mine Inspector : The term " district
mine inspector " means a person recommended by
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
and commissioner by the Governor of the State . He
is required to be certificated and is charged with the
inspection of mines and to see that the requirements
of this act are complied with .
Inspector of Mine Weights : The term " inspector
of mine weights " means a person having the same
qualifications as a district mine inspector . He is re-
quired to have a general knowledge of mine scales

432 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
and be thoroughly competent to inspect and test the
same .
Check Weighman : The term " check weighman "
means the person elected by a majority of the miners
engaged in digging or mining coal at a mine and see
that all coal mined or loaded by said miners is prop-
erly weighed and accounted for .
Approved Safety Lamps : The term " approved
safety lamp " shall mean any bonneted safety lamp
which bears the approval plate of the Federal Bu-
reau of Mines and is approved by the State Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals .
Gaseous Mine : A " gaseous mine" is one in
which the percentage of explosive gas exceeds one .
half of one per cent at the return of any one split
in a dusty mine and exceeds one and one -fourth per
cent at the return of any one split in a nondusty
maine .
Commercial Mine : The term " commercial mine "
means any coal mine from which coal is mined for
sale or exchange .
Tipple or Dumping Point : The term " tipple or
dumping point " means the tipple or structure
where coal is dumped or unloaded from the mine
car into railroad cars , trucks , wagons or other means
of conveyance .
The provisions of this act shall apply to all com-
mercial coal mines and where one ( six ) or more
persons are employed in a period of twenty -four
hours , the operator of any such mine shall be re-
quired to employ a certified mine foreman therein to
properly carry out the plans and rules when ap-
proved by the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals and to see that the mining law is com-
plied with as required in this act .
§ 2. Duties of the Department of Mines and
Minerals .- The supervision of all laws of the Com-

CHAPTER 100 433
monwealth of Kentucky relating to mines is vested
in the Department of Mines and Minerals .
§ 3. Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals, His Designation , Appointment , Term of Office ,
Salary and Expenses .-The chief officer of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals shall be designated
as the " Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals ." The salary of the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals shall be $4,200.00 per
annum , payable monthly as other salaries are paid
and he shall likewise be allowed and paid his neces-
sary traveling and other expenses incurred on ac-
count of and when engaged in the discharge of his
official duties . Immediately after the effective date
of the act , the Governor of Kentucky shall appoint
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
who shall serve for a term of four years , subject to
removal by the Governor at his pleasure.
§ 4. Qualifications of the Chief of the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals .-The Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals shall be a male
citizen of Kentucky and shall be a competent per-
son, having had at least fifteen ( 15 ) years mining
experience , ten ( 10 ) of which having been in and
about the mines in this state and he shall be thor-
oughly familiar with all methods of safety pertain-
ing to the operation of mines , he shall have a practi-
cal knowledge of the different systems of working
and ventilating coal mines and of the nature , chem-
istry , and properties of noxious and poisonous
gases , also of explosive gases , also of the dangers
due to such gases and the prevention thereof and
of the dangers incident to blasting and the preven-
tion thereof ; of the methods for the prevention of
explosions in mines due to gas or dust ; of the
methods for the management and extinguishing of
mine fires ; of the methods for rescue and relief

434 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
work in mine disasters ; of the application of
electricity in mining operations ; of the ap-
plication of mechanical loading in mining opera-
tions , also of equipment and explosives manu-
factured for use in coal mines ; of the mechanical
features of mine scales and the methods used in
properly inspecting and testing same ; of the
methods used in locating oil and gas wells when
drilled through any of the coal seams within the
state ; also the proper manner of drilling and plug-
ging of said oil and gas wells ; he shall have a practi-
cal knowledge of mining engineering and be able
to efficiently report on any proposed development
in mining operations or the possibilities of operat-
ing any of the coal seams within the state .
§ 5. Oath and Bond .-- Said Chief of the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals shall before he enters
upon the discharge of his duties , be sworn to dis-
charge said duties faithfully and impartially , which
oath shall be in writing and certified by the officer
administering it , and said oath and certificate shall
be filed in the office of the Secretary of State and
said Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
shall give bond in the penal sum of two thousand
dollars , with surety, to be approved by the Governor
for the faithful discharge of his duties . Said Chief
of the Department of Mines and Minerals shall be
liable to dismissal by the Governor for wilful neglect
of duty , or misconduct or malfeasance in office .
§ 6. Offices .- The main offices of the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals shall be located in the
Mining Engineering Building of the State Univer-
sity at Lexington , where adequate offices shall be
furnished it on account of the mutual problems the
department may have with the Mining Department
of the University , also the facilities of the Mining
Laboratory . The Chief of the Department of Mines

CHAPTER 100 435
and Minerals shall have the necessary engineering ,
stenographic and clerical assistance to properly
and efficiently carry out the duties of his office . The
offices of the respective district inspectors shall be
at such places within their distircts as the Chief of
the Department of Mines and Minerals may desig-
nate and they shall be allowed office rent not to ex-
ceed twenty dollars ( $20.00 ) a month in the discre-
tion of the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals .
§ 7. Duties of the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals .- Said Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals shall have full authority over
said department and he shall superintend and direct
the activities of the various inspectors and other per-
sonnel of the department . He shall have authority
to divide the coal fields of the state from time to
time into such number of inspection districts as may
be necessary to accord with the number of inspec-
tors in such manner as to equalize as nearly as
practicable the work of each inspector and to as-
sign to the inspectors their appropriate districts .
Whenever the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals may deem it necessary in the interest
of efficient supervision of the mines to temporarily
employ the services of two or more inspectors or
whenever he may deem it desirable in the interest
of efficient inspection to change inspectors from one
district to another he shall have the authority to do
so . He shall superintend and direct the inspection
of mines as herein provided and he shall cause to
be investigated the character and quality of air in
mines whenever conditions indicate the necessity of
so doing ; he shall collect statistics relating to coal
mining within the state each year and make report
of same ; see that maps , plans , projections and pro-
posed developments of all coal mines are made and

436 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
filed in his office , subject to his approval , according
to the provisions of this act ; keep a record of all
inspections made by himself and the different mem-
bers of the department , which shall be a permanent
record properly indexed and he shall perform all
other duties elsewhere in this act assigned to him.
He shall also have general supervision of the train-
ing of officials and workmen in safety and first aid
and mine rescue methods and the conducting of
demonstrations in safety when deemed advisable by
him to do so ; also the dissemination of information
among officials and employees concerning mine
ventilation , mining methods and mine accidents and
their prevention, assume full charge in the event of
mine fire or explosion or other serious accident at
any mine within the state . He may, in his discre-
tion , assist in the resumption of operations of any
mine or gather data for the development of any
coal seams that would be of any henefit to the state
or create employment for persons not already em-
ployed .
§ 8. Stationery , Postage , Etc.- The Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals and the various
inspectors shall be provided with all necessary
stationery and postage for the conduct of official
correspondence as other state officers are supplied .
The Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
shall be supplied with all printed blank forms ,
notices and other printed matter necessary for use
in the performance of his duties , upon his requisi-
tion therefor through the Superintendent of Public
Printing .
§ 9. There shall be provided for the Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals and all inspec-
tors such instruments , appliances , apparatus , chem-
icals , and vehicles as are necessary for the proper
discharge of his official duties , which shall be paid

CHAPTER 100 437
for on the order of the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals and which shall belong to the
state . The cost of repairs to such instruments , ap-
paratus and vehicles as may be necessary from time
to time shall be paid for by the inspector having
such repairs made , and be included in his account of
expenses incurred on account of official duties and
paid as they are paid .
§ 10. Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals Shall Furnish Certified Copy of Inspection
Report. The Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals shall furnish to any person a certified copy
of the report of inspection of any mine inspected
by himself or by a district inspector, when ordered
to do so by any circuit court judge , which copy shall
be admissable in evidence in any court in this Com-
monwealth, and shall be prima facie evidence of the
truth of the recitals therein contained .
§ 11. Annual Report of the Department of Mines
and Minerals .- The Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals shall annually make report to
the Governor of the Commonwealth his proceedings
for and during the calendar year ending on the
thirty -first day of December . Said annual report
shall be prepared and printed within four months.
and as much earlier as possible after the close of the
calendar year . Said annual report shall list the
complete operation of the coal mines in this state ,
enumerating all accidents which have occurred in or
about the same , and giving such other information
as said Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals may deem useful , and making such suggestions
as he may deem important as to further legislation
on the subject of mining .
§ 12. Operator's Monthly Report . For the pur-
pose of enabling the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals to keep records and make re-

438 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
ports as is required by this act , the owner , lessee or
superintendent of every mine to which this law ap-
plies is hereby required to give , each month , accu-
rate information on blanks to be furnished by the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals as
to all accidents that have occurred , number of per-
sons employed ; amount of coal mines ; and such
other information not of a private nature as the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
may request .
§ 13. Operator's Annual Report . The owner ,
lessee , or superintendent of each mine to which this
law applies shall also be required to give at the end
of each calendar year ending December thirty-first
accurate information on blanks to be furnished by
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals ,
as to the number of accidents that have occurred ,
the number of persons employed , the tons of coal
mined at such mine , and such other information not
of a private nature as the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals may request .
14. Mine Rescue Divisions .-The Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals is hereby author-
ized and directed to partition the coal fields of the
state into divisions which shall be known as mine
rescue divisions of which there shall be not less
than two in the western coal field and not less than
four in the eastern coal field . In each division there
shall be a station , the location of which shall be de-
termined by the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals , at which station shall be kept
apparatus , appliances and supplies for use in the
work of rescue and relief upon the occurrence of ex-
plosions or mine fires , and for the training of of-
ficials and miners in such work .
The Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals is hereby directed to provide each station , as

CHAPTER 100 439
it shall be located , and himself with such instru-
ments , appliances , apparatus , chemicals , and trucks
as may be necessary and he shall designate one of
his district inspectors to have charge of each sta-tion.
The supplies and repairs necessary for the in-
struments , appliances , apparatus , chemicals , and
trucks and other equipment shall be purchased by
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
as needed from time to time and paid for as other
expenses are paid , on the order of said Chief of
the Department of Mines and Minerals .
§ 15. Governor to appoint District Inspectors
and Inspectors of Mine Weights ; Also Their
Qualifications . The Governor shall appoint not ex-
ceeding ten district mine inspectors and not exceed-

which must have been in and about the mines in
this state . Said district inspectors and inspectors

in any official capacity in operating any coal mine
and he shall be of good moral character and tem-

of four years , subject to removal by the Governor
at his pleasure , and whose salaries shall not be less
than $2,400.00 dollars nor more than $2,700.00 dol-
lars per annum , payable monthly and shall likewise
be allowed and paid their necessary traveling and
other expenses incurred on account of and when
engaged in the discharge of their official duties . Said
salaries to be determined by the Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals and approval by
the Governor and based on ability and experience
of the inspector . Said district inspectors and in-
spectors of mine weights before entering upon the
discharge of their duties , shall be sworn to discharge
those duties faithfully and impartially and to the
best of their skill and ability , which oath shall be
certified by the officer administering it and said oath ,
in writing, and certificate shall be filed in the office
of the Secretary of State , each of said district in-
spectors and inspectors of mine weights shall give
hond in the penal sum of one thousand dollars ( $1 ,-
000.00 ) with surety to be approved by the Governor
for the faithful discharge of his official duties .
§ 16. Certification of District Mine Inspectors.—
Before any person shall be appointed to the office
of district mine inspector , he shall be required to
pass a satisfactory examination before the Board
of Examiners hereinafter in this act provided for .
The Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals
is hereby authorized to recommend to the Governor
of the state applicants for district mine inspector

CHAPTER 100 441
who have successfully passed such examination and
who are proved by worth , training and experience
to be the most competent of such applicants .
17. Duties of District Mine Inspectors .- Each
of said district inspectors shall give his entire time
and attention to the duties of his office which shall
consist of the inspection of coal mines , and of aiding
under the direction of the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals , in carrying out and enforc-

442 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY

such information concerning the mines and mining
conditions in his district as may be appropriate and
required by the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals . He shall visit each mine in his dis-
trict at least one ( once ) every three months , or
oftener if practicable to do so , and make a personal
examination of the interior of each mine with re-
spect to ventilation , drainage , timbering , blasting ,
electricity and general security as well as the out-
side of the mine , where any danger to the workmen
may exist , and he shall see that all provisions of this
act are strictly carried out .
$ 19. Ventilation of Mines .-Should the district
inspector discover any room, entry , airway or other
working places being driven in advance of the air
currents contrary to the requirements of this act , he
shall notify the mine foreman to cease work in such
places at once until the law is complied with .
The ventilation of all coal mines at which as many
as five men are employed shall be produced by means
of fans mechanically operated , located outside the
mine . Mines at which less than five men are em-
ployed shall be ventilated by means approved by
the Department of Mines and Minerals . Provided ,
however , that when inside fans are required to ven-
tilate working places on longwall or other modern
systems of mining , the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals must first approve such in-
stallation .
In case of accident to the ventilating fan , or its
machinery whereby the ventilation of the mine
would be seriously interrupted , the mine foreman
shall order the men to withdraw immediately from
the mine and he shall not allow them to return to
their work until the ventilation has been restored

CHAPTER 100 443
and the mine has been thoroughly examined by him ,
or by an assistant mine foreman or fire boss , and
the mine is reported safe .
Any operator, or superintendent of a coal mine
before making any new or additional openings , from
the outside , shall submit to the Chief of the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals , for his information
and approval , a map or plan showing the proposed
system of mining the coal seam and the proposed
system of ventilation and equipment of the openings ·
with their locations and relative positions to ad-
jacent developments . In order that the records of the
Department of Mines and Minerals be complete as to
all operating mines the operator or superintendent
of a coal mine who has not previous to the passage
of this Act submitted to the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals a map or plan showing the
proposed system of mining and the proposed system
of ventilation and equipment of the openings with
their locations and relative positions to adjacent de-
velopments , shall for his information and approval
submit a map or plan forthwith ; and no such new or
additional openings shall be made until approved by
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals .
�
The operator, superintendent or mine foreman of
every coal mine , whether worked by shaft , slope or
drift , shall provide and hereafter maintain for every
such mine adequate ventilation affording no (not )
less than one hundred cubic feet of air per minute
for each and every person employed in the portion
of the mine affected thereby and two hundred cubic
feet of air for each animal working in the part of
such mine affected thereby , which shall be circulated
around the main headings and cross headings and
room headings and working places to an extent that
will dilute , render harmless and carry off any noxi-
ous and dangerous gases therein . As working places

444 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
advance , break throughs for air shall be made sixty
feet apart , except where companies now are per-
mitted to drive with the approval of the District In-
spector and the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals to ninety feet before turning break
throughs . Provided however , where longwall or
modern systems of mining are employed the Chief
of the Department of Mines and Minerals shall have
the authority to approve , at his discretion , a greater.
distance between break throughs or the method of
properly ventilating such longwall or modern sys-
tem of mining . All the break throughs between the
intake and return airways not required for the pas-
sage of air shall be closed with stoppings sub-
stantially built with suitable material , which shall be
approved by the district inspector, so as to keep
working places well ventilated.
As many as sixty persons shall be permitted to
work in the same air current , or split , provided how-
ever , that with the approval of the district inspector
and the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals , as many as eighty persons may be per-
mitted to work in the same air current , or split .
No operator , superintendent or mine foreman
shall permit any persons to work where they are
unable to maintain a sufficient amount of ventilation ,
but this shall not be construed to prohibit the
operator from employing men to make the places of
employment safe to comply with this requirement ;
provided , further , that while the repair work neces-
sary to get the mine in condition to comply with the
law is in progress , no person or persons shall be per-
mitted to enter that part of the mine affected except
those actually employed in doing the necessary re-
pair work , said repair work to be done under the
constant supervision of an official designated by the
mine foreman .

pound that in the opinion of the inspector will con-
taminate the air to such an extent as to be injurious
to the health of the miner shall be used as motive
power in any mine .

20.Accumulation
ofCoal Dust
.
—

accumulations
offine dry coal dust shall

446 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
practicable , be removed from the mine and all dry
and dusty operating sections kept thoroughly
watered or rock dusted or dust allayed by such other
methods as may be approved by the Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals .
§ 21. Shafts , Slopes and Drifts .-It shall not be
lawful for the operator, superintendent , or mine
foreman of any coal mine , whether worked by shaft ,
slope or drift , to permit persons to work therein un-
less there are to every seam of coal worked in such
mine at least two separate outlets , separated by a
natural strata of not less than one hundred feet in
breadth if the mine be worked by shaft or slope , and
separated by natural strata of not less than fifty
feet in breadth if the mine be worked by drift-by
which outlets distinct means of ingress and egress
are readily available to persons employed in such
mine ; but it shall not be necessary for the two out-
lets to belong to the same mine .
The foregoing requirements shall not apply to the
openings of a new entry of a mine that is being
worked for the purpose of making connection be-
tween said two outlets so long as not more than four
persons are employed at one time in making the con-
nection or driving the second opening ; nor shall said
requirements apply to any mine in which the second
opening has been rendered unavailable by reason of
final robbing or removal of pillars so long as not
more than twenty persons are employed therein at
one time.
In case any coal mine has but one shaft , slope , or
drift for ingress and egress of the persons employed
therein, and the owner thereof does not own suitable
ground for another opening , such owner may select
appropriate associate adjacent ground for that pur-
pose and have the same condemned and appropriate
the same by proceedings in the county court of the

CHAPTER 100 447 ·
county wherein the mine is situated similar to pro-
ceedings now allowed for securing a private pass-
way .
In every shaft used as an escape outlet there shall
be provided a stairway or ladderway of suitable
strength , design , and angle , which shall be provided
with platforms or landings at each turn of the stair-
way or ladderway , the whole to be subject to the ap-
proval of the district inspector and the Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals , or in lieu of
such stairway or ladderway there shall be provided
a properly equipped hoisting arrangement , consist-
ing of a cage suitable for hoisting men , a wire hoist-
ing rope of ample strength, and an adequate and
safe hoisting engine and drum, which hoisting ar-
rangement shall be ready for use at any time of
emergency . When the escape and fan shafts are
combined , the side or end used for an escape outlet
and the part used for ventilating purposes shall be
separated by a good , substantial air-tight partition
from top to bottom , so that the use of the escape
shaft shall not in any way interfere with the ventila-
tion of the mine . All shafts by which men enter or
leave the mine and the passageways to escape out-
lets shall be carefully examined by the mine fore-
man , or by a person designated by him , at least once
each week that the mine may be operating, and the
date and findings of such examination shall be
entered in a book kept at the mine for that purpose .
No debris , such as falls of slate and similar material ,
which shall act as an obstruction to the free passage
of men , shall be allowed to accumulate in the pas-
sageway to the escape outlet , nor shall bodies of
water difficult to pass be allowed to accumulate
there , nor shall the passageway be obstructed by
mine cars or timbers . If obstructions of any sort to
the free passage of men be found , such obstruction

448 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
shall be promptly removed . At points where the pas-
sageway to the escape outlet is intersected by road-
ways or entries calculated to produce doubt as to the
proper direction to the outlet , conspicuous sign-
boards shall be placed indicating the direction it is
necessary to take to reach the exit (for) escape .
At every mine operated by shaft , there shall be
provided an approved safety catch , and a sufficient
and substantial cover overhead on all cages used for
lowering or hoisting persons , and at the top of each
shaft a safety gate shall be provided . An adequate
brake , so adjusted that it may be operated by the
engineer without leaving his post at the levers , shall
be attached to every drum or equivalent machine
used for the lowering or raising persons at all shafts
or slopes , and an efficient indicator or dial shall be
used in connection with the hoisting engine which
will show the engineer the positions of the cages in
the shaft at any time ; and approved means for
signaling to the bottom man , the top man , and engi-
neer shall be provided and all shafts used for lower-
ing or hoisting men shall be equipped with metal
tubes , pipes , or telephone , through which conversa-
tions may be freely held between persons at the bot-
tom and top landings . No cages on which men are
riding shall be lifted or lowered at a rate of speed
greater than six hundred feet per minute . The
hoisting rope , the safety catches and the cage attach-
ments shall be examined by the mine foreman or
some competent person designated by him , every
morning before the men descend into the mine ; and
care shall be taken to keep them in good working
condition at all times . All persons are prohibited
from riding on cages when coal or slate or simliar
material is being raised , and no person shall enter
a cage at the bottom to be raised to the top during
the running hours of the mine , or when leaving work

CHAPTER 100 449
at the close of the day's run , without first being
authorized by the bottom -man or cager to do So , said
bottom -man or cager having first signaled to the
engineer that men are to be raised . At every under-
ground landing where persons enter or leave the
cage and where persons must pass from one side of
the shaft to the other there shall be a passageway
not less than three feet wide and of suitable height ,
kept free from obstruction , and as dry as possible ,
around the shaft ; and all employes when passing
from one side of the shaft to the other side shall use
passageway only. Whenever the hoisting or lower-
ing of men occurs before daylight or after dark , and
when the landing at which men take or leave the
cage is obscured by steam or otherwise , there shall
be maintained at each landing a light sufficient to
show the landing and objects in immediate proximity
thereto , and as long as men are underground a good
light shall be maintained at the bottom of the shaft ,
so that persons coming to the bottom may discern
the cage and objects in the vicinity thereof .
§ 22. Checking Employees in and Out of Mine.-
The operator or Superintendent of every mine shall
install a uniform system of checking the employees
in and out of the mine , whereby each employee upon
entering the mine shall be given a check by which he
shall be identified , and which check shall be placed
in its proper place on the check board by the em-
ployee when leaving the mine .
§ 23. Shaft , Slope or Incline .-No operator or
superintendent of any coal mine worked by shaft ,
slope or incline shall place in charge of any engine
or drum used for lowering or hoisting any persons
employed in such mine any but competent and sober
engineers or drum runners ; and no engineer in
charge of such machinery shall allow any person
except such as may be designated for this purpose
Acts-15

450 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
by the operator or superintendent to interfere with
any part of the machinery ; and no person shall
interfere with or intimidate the engineer or drum
runner in the discharge of his duties and in no case
where the mine is operated or worked by shaft , slope
or incline shall more than ten persons ride in any
cage or car at one time , without the approval of the
district inspector and the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals first having been obtained ,
and no person shall ride on a loaded cage or car in
any shaft or slope or on any incline . Provided how-
ever , where special man cars or cages are employed
to haul workmen on inclines the Chief of the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals shall have the authority
to regulate the method and procedure of said hand-
ling of man cars or workmen .
§ 24. First Aid Equipment .-In every mine
where fifty or a less number of men are employed
underground, it shall be the duty of the operator
thereof to keep always on hand at the mine one prop-
erly constructed stretcher , one woolen and water-
proof blanket , and all necessary requisites which
may be advised by the Department of Mines and
Minerals and for each additional fifty men so em-
ployed , one additional stretcher and equipment as
above specified shall be furnished .
§ 25. Scope of Inspection , Etc.- The operator
or superintendent of every coal mine shall furnish
the inspector proper facilities for entering such mine
and making examinations or obtaining information ;
and if any inspector shall discover that any mine
does not in respect to appliances for the safety of
the persons employed therein conform to the pro-
visions of this act , or that by reason of any defect
or practice in or at or about such mine the lives or
health of persons employed therein , are endangered ,
he shall immediately orally and in writing notify

CHAPTER 100 451
such operator or superintendent thereof, stating in
such notice the particulars in which he considers
such mine to be defective or dangerous and if he
deems it necessary for the protection of the lives
and health of the persons employed in such mine he
shall , after giving notice to the said operator or
superintendent , in writing , notify immediately the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals .
The Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals shall forthwith summons the operator ,
superintendent , and the district inspector to discuss
at the mine the unsafe practices , defects , or un-
healthful conditions in the mine and if the Chief of
the Department of Mines and Minerals determines
that for the safety of the persons employed in said
mine the mine should be closed , he shall forthwith
order the mine to be closed until it is placed in a safe
and proper condition for mining operations ; the
owner or operator of any mine so closed may apply
to the circuit court wherein such mine is located , or
the judge thereof in vacation , by petition for an
order directing said mine to be re -opened , and such
court , or the judge thereof in vacation , shall imme-diately hear and determine the matters arising upon
such petition , and if upon full hearing thereof the
court , or the judge thereof in vacation shall find that
said mine is in a reasonable safe condition the
prayer of said petition shall be granted ; but notice
of said hearing shall be given to the district mine
inspector and the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals three days at the least before said
hearing ; and in all such hearings the Attorney Gen-
eral, the Commonwealth's Attorney , or the County
Attorney of the District or County in which the mine
is situated , shall appear for the state and defend thesame .

452 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
§ 26. Approved Closed Lights , When to Be Used.
-In any mine or sections of mines liberating ex-
plosive gas where the lives of the persons employed
therein may be endangered , by the liberating of said
explosive gas the district inspector with the ap-
proval of the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals may direct the operator , or superintendent
of any mine or mines to work said mine or mines ex-
clusively by the use of approved safety lamps and
approved electric lamps . The operator or super-
intendent of said mine or mines shall forthwith
carry out the recommendations of the district mine
inspector when so approved by the Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals .
In mines where flame safety lamps and approved
electric lamps are required to be used for working
therein , the lamps shall be in charge of some person
to be designated by the operator or superintendent ;
and at least two additional flame safety lamps shall
be kept at every coal mine whether such mine
liberates explosive gas or not. If deemed advisable
by the district inspector and the Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals all mine foremen
and fire bosses employed in gaseous mines shall , at
all times , carry an approved flame safety lamp for
the purpose of detecting the presence of explosive
gas , said lamp to be kept lighted at all times when in
use inside the mines .
In mines where approved safety lamps and ap-
proved electric lamps are used , no miner , workman ,
or other person shall at any time enter any mine or
carry therein any intoxicating liquors , matches ,
pipes , cigars , cigarettes , or any device for making
lights or fire not authorized or approved ; and in all
such mines the operator shall , at frequent intervals ,
search or cause to be searched , any person entering
or about to enter any mine , to prevent such persons

.-No operator
, super-

ahorse

454 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
in writing by the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals .
$ 30. Steam Locomotive .-No steam locomotive
shall be used in any mine in this state unless it be
approved in writing by the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals .
§ 31. Explosives , Magazines , Where to be
Located . All distributing explosives magazines
used for quantities of explosives estimated as a
daily supply shall be located not less than one hun-
dred feet from any mine opening or building used
or occupied by any person or persons ; and the out-
side construction of such magazines shall be of non-
combustible material .
All magazines used for storing powder or other
explosives in greater quantities than an estimated
daily supply shall be located not less than five hun-
dred feet from any mine opening or building used or
occupied by any person or persons ; and the outside
construction of such magazines shall be of non-com-
bustible material , provided however , this shall not
apply to distributing or storage magazines already
constructed prior to the passage of this act .
Where Cardox or other explosives of a similar
nature are manufactured at or near the mine , the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals shall
have the authority to prescribe the location and pro-
cedure of such installation .
§ 32. First Aid Training.-It shall be the duty
of each and every coal operator in this state to see
that all employees receive the necessary first aid
training for the safety of themselves and fellow-
workmen as recommended by the State Department
of Mines and Minerals . It shall be the duty of each
employee to receive the necessary training .
$ 33. Electricity in Mines .- On all haulage roads ,
landings and partings , where men are required

CHAPTER 100 455
to regularly work or pass under trolley or other
bare power wires which are placed less than six and
one-half feet above top of rail , a suitable protection
shall be provided . This protection shall consist of
channeling the roof , placing boards along the wires ,
which shall extend below it , or the use of other ap-
proved devices that afford protection .
All machine feed wires shall be placed on glass or
porcelain insulators which shall be so placed as to
prevent the wires coming in contact with the coal .
When machine or feed wires are carried in same
entry as trolley wire , they shall be placed on the
same side as the trolley wire , between trolley wire
and rib and protected from contact therewith , and
positive feed wires crossing places where persons
or animals are required to travel , shall be safely
guarded or protected from such persons or animals
coming in contact therewith, as provided in this
section .
All trolley and positive feed wires shall be placed
on opposite sides of track from refuge holes or necks
of room , when so ordered by the Department of
Mines and Minerals , and wires may be placed across
the necks of rooms when protected as provided for
in this section. Switches or circuit -breakers shall
be provided to control the current at the mine , and
all important sections in the mine .
All power wires and cables in hoisting shafts or
manway compartments shall be properly insulated ,
substantially fixed and well protected .
$ 34. Locomotives in Gaseous Mines .-In gase-
ous mines the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals may designate where flame -proof electric
locomotives shall be used .
$ 35. Electric Coal- Cutting Machines .-In gase-
ous mines the Chief of the Department of Mines and

456 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Minerals may designate where flame-proof electric
coal -cutting machines shall be used .
No man shall be placed in charge of a coal -cutting
machine in any gaseous mine or gaseous portion of
a mine who is not a competent person, capable of
determining the safety of the roof and the sides of
the working places and detecting the presence of ex-
plosive gas . Machine runners shall be compelled to
undergo an examination to determine their fitness
to detect explosive gas before they are permitted to
have charge of machines in mines liberating gas ,
unless they be accompanied by a certified fire boss ,
or a machine runner or helper having passed such
an examination . Said examination to be given by
the mine foreman and approved by the District In-
spector , blank forms for same to be furnished by the
Department of Mines and Minerals , a copy to be
retained on file at the mine office and the original to
be sent to the State Department of Mines and Min-
erals fully made out and signed by the machine run-
ner and the mine foreman and approved by the
District Inspector .
In any gaseous portion of a mine , a coal - cutting
machine shall not be brought within the last break
through next the working face , until the machine
man shall have made an inspection for gas in the
place where the machine is to work , unless such ex-
amination is then made by some other competent
person authorized and appointed for that purpose
by the mine foreman . If any explosive gas is found
in the place the machine shall not be taken in until
the gas is removed .
In working places where gas is likely to be en-
countered , a safety lamp , or other suitable approved
apparatus for the detection of fire damp , shall be
provided for use with each machine when working ,
and should any indication of fire damp appear on

CHAPTER 100 457
the flame of the safety lamp , or other apparatus
used for the detection of fire damp , the person in
charge shall immediately stop the machine , cut off
the current at the nearest switch, and report the
matter to the mine foreman , or fire boss , and the
machine shall not again be started in such place
until the mine foreman , fire boss , or a person duly
authorized by either has examined it and pronounced
it safe . All coal - cutting machines in mines where
fire damp is detected , shall be provided with a box
specially designed for carrying safety lamps for the
protection of such lamps.
No coal -cutting machine shall be operated in a
gaseous portion of a mine for a longer period than
ten minutes without an examination as above de-
scribed being made for gas , and if gas is found , the
current shall at once be switched off the machine ,
and the trailing cable shall forthwith be disconnect-
ed from the power supply .
$ 36 . Fire Boss and His Duties .- In all gaseous
mines as defined in this Act the operator or super-
intendent shall employ a fire boss or fire bosses ,
whose competency to act as such shall be evidenced
by a certificate of qualification from the Board of
Examiners as provided in this Act . It shall be the
duty of the fire boss to examine carefully , before
each shift enters the mine , every working place with-
out exception , all places adjacent to live workings ,
every roadway, every unfenced road to abandoned
workings , and falls in the mine , but before proceed-
ing with the examination he shall see that the air
current is traveling its proper course . In making
the examination he shall use no light other than that
inclosed in an approved safety lamp . The fire boss
shall examine for all dangers in all portions of the
mine under his charge , and after examination he
shall leave at the face of every place examined the

458 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
date of the examination , as evidence that he has
performed his duty . He shall also examine the en-
trance or entrances to all worked -out and aban-
doned portions adjacent to the roadways and work-
ing places under his charge where explosive gas is
likely to accumulate , and he shall place a danger
signal across the entrance to every working place
and every other place where explosive gas is dis-
covered or where immediate danger is found to exist
from any other cause , and said signal shall be suf-
ficient warning for persons not to enter .
A suitable record book shall be kept at the mine
office of every mine wherein fire bosses are em
ployed , and immediately after the examination of
such mine or any portion thereof , by a fire boss ,
whose duty it is to make such examination , he shall
enter in said book with ink or indelible pencil , a
record of such examination , and sign same . This
record shall show the time taken in making the ex-
amination , and also clearly state the nature and lo-
cation of any danger that may have been discovered
in any room or entry or other place in the mine . If
any danger or damages ( dangers ) has or have been
discovered , the fire bosses shall immediately report
the location thereof to the mine forman , or in his
absence to the assistant mine foreman in charge ,
who in turn shall report to the mine foreman . No
person shall enter the mine until the fire bosses re-
turn to the mine office , or to a station located in the
intake entry of the mine (where a record book as
provided for in this Section shall be kept and signed
by the person making the examination ) and report to
the mine foreman or the assistant mine foreman , by
telephone or otherwise , that the mine is in safe
condition for the men to enter . When a station is
located in any mine it shall be the duty of the fire
bosses to sign also the report entered in the record

CHAPTER 100 459
book in the mine office on the surface . The record
books of the fire bosses shall at all times during
working hours be accessible to the District In-
spector .
The mine foreman and the fire boss shall at or
near the main entrance to the mine , provide a per-
manent station with a proper danger signal desig-
nated by suitable letters and colors placed thereon .
In every mine in which fire bosses are required to be
employed by this Act, when the working portions
are one mile or more from the entrance to the mine
or from the bottom of the shaft or slope , a station
of suitable dimensions may be erected by the mine
foreman (provided the location is approved by the
District Inspector ) for the use of the fire bosses . It
shall not be lawful for any person, except the mine
foreman , and in case of necessity such other persons
as may be designated by him , to pass beyond said
station and danger signal until the mine has been
examined by a fire boss , as provided in this Sec-
tion, and the mine or certain portions thereof re-
ported by him to be safe . The fire boss shall not
allow any other person or persons to enter or re-
main in any portion of the mine through which a
dangerous accumulation of gas is being passed into
the ventilating current from any portion of the
mine . He shall report any violation of this section
to the mine foreman at once.
Any employee or other person, except those here-
in above provided for , who passes any danger signal
to the mine , or into any portion of the mine or re-
moves such danger signal before the mine has been
examined and reported to be safe , or any employee
or any other person who passes any danger signal
placed at the entrance to a working place , or any
other place in the mine , or remove such danger
signal without permission from the mine foreman ,

460 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
the assistant , or the fire boss shall be deemed guilty
of a misdemeanor , and it shall be the duty of the
mine foreman having knowledge of such violation
(whether obtained personally or otherwise ) to
notify the operator or superintendent at once of
such violation , who shall in turn notify the District
Inspector , in writing , and the District Inspector
shall forthwith institute proceedings against such
person or persons as provided for in this Act.
Any fire boss who neglects to comply with the
provisions of this Act relating to his duties , or who
shall make a false report of the condition of any
place in the portion of the mine allotted to him for
examination , shall be deemed guilty of a misde-
meanor , and shall be suspended by the mine fore-
man , and his name shall be given to the District
Inspector for prosecution . If he be found guilty he
shall return his certificate of qualification as fire
boss to the Department of Mines and Minerals .
Nothing in this Article shall prevent a mine fore-
man holding a certificate of the first class from act-
ing as fire boss , or a regularly employed fire boss
from acting in an emergency as a mine foreman
holding such first class certificate .
§ 37. Shot Firers .-In any coal mine in this
state wherein the operator may choose to employ
practical and experienced persons as shot firers to
prepare the cartridge and set off and discharge the
shots he may do so .
Provided , however , in gaseous mines the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals may require the oper-
ator to employ practical and experienced persons as
shot firers and the Department of Mines and Min-
erals may prescribe the conditions under which
blasting may be carried on and the kind of explo-
sives that shall be used .

CHAPTER 100 461

§39. Some Duties
ofOperator
orSuperin-
tendent
-Mine Supplies
.

bethe duty
of
every superintendent
, on behalf and at the expense
ofthe operator
,
tokeep on hand
ator within con-
venient distance of each mine at all times

quantity
ofall materials and supplies required
to

, as ordered by

.

any reason the superintendent cannot procure the

, he

, or

, read and examine carefully

inthe mine

.

oftheir duties

,
